evidence.text: The hydrothermal solutions could have been derived from the enclosing metasediments during metamorphism.
evidence.paragraph_text: Swayel deposit PGM are represented by Pd bismuthotellurides which are considered to be characteristic of low-temperature hydrothermal deposition in other deposits, such as New Rambler mine, Wyoming (McCallum et al., 1976); the Bucko Lake intrusion, Thompson Belt, Manitoba (Good and Naldrett, 1993); and the Sudbury area (Farrow and Watkinson, 1992). PGM from the Abu Swayel deposit are interpreted as products of precipitation of PGE from hydrothermal solutions of decreasing temperature after the peak of amphibolite facies metamorphism. The hydrothermal solutions could have been derived from the enclosing metasediments during metamorphism. The movement of these solutions was facilitated along the synmetamorphic shear zone. The palladian bismuthian melonite inclusions in cubanite and chalcopyrite represent the early stage of mineralization. The associated sphalerite and cubanite exsolutions indicate formation slightly after peak metamorphic conditions, i.e., ${ > } 5 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (Yund and Kullerud, 1966; Kojima and Sugaki, 1985). At the last stage of solidification of Cu-rich base metal sulfides, merenskyite and michenerite precipitated at the contact beween base metal sulfide and hydrosilicates. Experimental evidence shows that michenerite may coexist with merenskyite at $4 7 5 \mathrm { { } ^ { \circ } C }$ (Hofman and MacLean, 1976). Precipitation of froodite in association with electrum and hessite in hypogene violarite and pyrrhotite occurred at a slightly lower temperature. The close association with hypogene violarite and pyrrhotite suggests temperatures between ${ \sim } 4 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ and $2 5 0 \mathrm { { ^ \circ C } }$ (Craig, 1971; Kissin, 1974). The hydrothermal solutions were still enriched in PGE, Bi, Te, Au, and Sb and froodite, michenerite, and sudburyite were formed along fractures on the base metal sulfide and the Ni-rich merenskyite in association with joseite, altaite, and electrum on fractures in metamorphic plagioclase and almandine-rich garnet associated with valleriite. The paragenesis of PGM and related minerals is shown in Figure 7. For the magmatic realm, quantitative experimental data have recently (Ballhaus and Ulmer, 1995) become available demonstrating that the association of base metal sulfides with PGM in the Merensky reef, South Africa, is a low-temperature phenomenon. Equilibration of exsolved PGM associations with sulfides occurs down to temperatures of $1 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The PGM observed here may represent a metamorphic corollary to these processes.
evidence.document_title: PLATINUM-GROUP MINERALS FROM THE METAMORPHOSED ABU SWAYEL Cu-Ni-PGE DEPOSIT, SOUTH EASTERN DESERT, EGYPT

evidence.text: Ore reserves are about 85,000 tons containing 2.8 percent Cu, 1.53 percent Ni, and minor amounts of Co.
evidence.paragraph_text: The Abu Swayel Cu-Ni deposit is located $1 8 5 \mathrm { k m }$ southeast of Aswan. It was discovered by the ancient Egyptians who exploited the oxidized top part to a depth of $1 0 \mathrm { m }$ by open pits. No mining activities have been recorded in modern times; however, geologic and feasibility studies were conducted by DEMAG of West Germany in 1960. This work included deepening the shaft to $6 9 ~ \mathrm { m }$ , but the results showed that the deposit is not economic. Ore reserves are about 85,000 tons containing 2.8 percent Cu, 1.53 percent Ni, and minor amounts of Co. Relevant studies on this area include those by Nassim (1943, 1949), Bassyouni (1960), El Goresy (1964), and El Shazly et al. (1969).

evidence.text: The Abu Swayel Cu-Ni deposit occurs in conformable, lenslike bodies of mafic-ultramafic rocks
evidence.paragraph_text: The Abu Swayel Cu-Ni deposit occurs in conformable, lenslike bodies of mafic-ultramafic rocks in Proterozoic metasediments. The mineralization and the enclosing rocks have been metamorphosed to amphibolite facies. Chalcopyrite, cubanite, pyrrhotite, pyrite, and violarite are the major sulfide minerals. At the peak of amphibolite facies metamorphism, the associated fuid regimes resulted in remobilization and transport of Cu-rich sulfides and PGE and in the development of hydrosilicate alteration zones. Six platinum-group minerals are described: michenerite, froodite, merenskyite, sudburyite, geversite, and palladian bismuthian melonite. Hessite, joseite, altaite, bismuthinite, and electrum are common associated minerals. Mineral assemblages and textural relations indicate precipitation of PGE during postmetamorphic cooling, over a wide range of temperatures. The presence of PGM on tension fractures in metamorphic plagioclase and almandine-rich garnet in association with $\mathrm { { \sf P b } }$ , Bi, and Ag tellurides illustrates the participation of hydrothermal solutions generated during amphibolite facies metamorphism in the transport and concentration of PGE. This also underlines the possible role of metamorphic processes in the formation of PGE deposits.
evidence.document_title: PLATINUM-GROUP MINERALS FROM THE METAMORPHOSED ABU SWAYEL Cu-Ni-PGE DEPOSIT, SOUTH EASTERN DESERT, EGYPT

evidence.text: The localization of bismuthotellurides at the syn-metamorphic shear zone at Abu Swayel
evidence.paragraph_text: Bi contents of the studied prospects were not analysed, the telluride assemblage in each area can give an idea about Te activity in the environment. The few telluride phases (only Ag, Ni and Pd tellurides) and the small grain size and low population of tellurides at Gabbro Akarem suggest low Te activity. The formation of a diverse range of tellurides (Ag, Ni, Pd, Pb, Bi tellurides) at Genina Gharbia and Abu Swayel and their high abundance suggest higher Te activity in both areas, Genina Gharbia being the richest (indicated by the crystallization of native Te). Afifi et al. (1988a) state that the fugacity of $\mathrm { T e } _ { 2 }$ is ultimately controlled by the supply of tellurium from the source. The low Te activity at Gabbro Akarem is probably a primary magmatic feature, i.e., the magma was Te depleted. Helmy (2004) suggested two sources of Te and Bi at Genina Gharbia, i.e., the evolved parent magma (e.g., Mulja and Mitchell, 1991), and the nearby metasedimentary rocks. The localization of bismuthotellurides at the syn-metamorphic shear zone at Abu Swayel suggests that metamorphic fluids introduced Te and Bi. Although these elements could be mobilized from the ultramafic rocks, input from a sedimentary source is indicated by the silicate assemblage (e.g., Gervilla et al., 1997). Before going on to discuss the reasons for compositional differences in MGM from the three deposits, the mechanism of telluride crystallization in each area is presented.

evidence.paragraph_text: The Wadi Allaqi district contains the western part of the Allaqi-Heiani belt in the South Eastern Desert. This belt was developed through a four-phase deformation history (Table 1), in which an early N–S to NNE–SSW regional shortening led to development of SSW-verging folds and NNE dipping thrusts (e.g., Abdelsalam and Stern 1996; Abdelsalam et al. 2003 and references therein). The shortening fabrics are imposed by NNW–SSE oriented folds and major wrench faults/shear zones, generally showing leftlateral displacement. These deformation events are interpreted as an early terrane accretion along the AllaqiHeiani through basin closure above a north-dipping subduction zone, and a later collision between East- and WestGondwana at ca. $7 5 0 { - } 6 5 0 \ \mathrm { M a }$ (Kusky and Ramadan 2002 and references therein). The late collision deformed the Allaqi-Heiani belt along N–S trending shortening zones and produced NW–SE sinistral and NE–SW dextral transpressional faults (Zoheir and Klemm 2007). Abdeen and Abdelghaffar (2011) suggested that the late collision phase was related to the Neoproterozoic Najd orogen $( 6 4 0 - 5 5 0 \mathrm { M a } )$ .
evidence.document_title: Trace elements and isotope data of the Um Garayat gold deposit, Wadi Allaqi district, Egypt

evidence.paragraph_text: In this paper, we focus on the gneisses, which represent a part of the Wadi Haimur–Abu Swayel gneiss belt. We discuss field relations, petrography, mineral chemistry and qualitative thermobarometric data, as well as $\mathrm { K } / \mathrm { A r }$ ages for these rocks. These results are important for understanding the geotectonic environment of these gneisses and provide constraints on the Neoproterozoic (Pan-African) crustal evolution of the southern part of the Eastern Desert of Egypt.

The ores appear to have behaved in a variably ductile manner during deformation, resulting in fractionation of Ni-rich from Cu-rich sulfides. The Ni-rich ores are relatively enriched in Os, Ir, Ru, Rh and Pd, whereas the most Cu-rich ores are depleted in all PGE.

evidence.text: it was estimated that the parental magmas to the intrusions were tholeiitic basalts
evidence.paragraph_text: The intrusion hosting the Phikwe deposit forms part of the Selebi-Phikwe belt of intrusions that also contain the Selebi, Selebi North, Dikoloti, Lentswe and Phokoje deposits (Fig. 1e–g). In all these deposits, the sulfide ores are mainly associated with boudinaged lenses and layers of fine- to medium-grained amphibolite interlayered with various types of gneisses (Gordon 1973; Wakefield 1976; Key 1976; Gallon 1986; Brown 1988). The ore-bearing intrusions are generally relatively thin (e.g. on average 11 m in the Phikwe area; Lear 1979), but this may largely be the result of intense folding and shearing. The amphibolites consist mainly of hornblende, feldspar, gedrite and mica. Minor metamorphic orthopyroxene and olivine also occur. Based on whole rock compositional data and CIPW norms of a large number of samples, Brown (1988) estimated that the parental magmas to the intrusions were tholeiitic basalts (with ca $8 \ \mathrm { \ w t { \% } \ M g O } ,$ that crystallised variable proportions of olivine $\left( \mathrm { F o } _ { 8 5 } \right)$ , pyroxene $\left( \mathrm { E n } _ { 8 5 } \right)$ and plagioclase $\left( \mathsf { A n } _ { 8 3 } \right)$ .

evidence.text: The Selkirk Formation also hosts the Phoenix, Selkirk and Tekwane meta-gabbronoritic intrusions
evidence.paragraph_text: The main lithologies within the Tati greenstone belt comprise lower greenschist to lower amphibolite facies volcanic and sedimentary rocks intruded by granitoids of unknown age (mainly tonalitic orthogneiss, tonalitic paragneiss, and post-tectonic tonalite–adamellite plutons; Fig. 1b). The volcano-sedimentary succession has been subdivided into three formations that contain a progressively higher proportion of felsic volcanic rocks with height (Key 1976). At the base is the $< 1 , 6 0 0 \mathrm { ~ m ~ }$ Lady Mary Formation that consists mainly of altered komatiite and komatiitic basalt and lesser amounts of quartzitic schist, limestone and iron formation. The overlying ${ \tt > } 1 0 \mathrm { - k m }$ -thick Penhalonga Formation consists of basaltic, andesitic and rhyolitic volcanics and volcaniclastic rocks, as well as phyllites, black shales (containing unspecified amounts of accessory pyrite, Key 1976, p 21), limestones and jaspilites. This is capped by the Selkirk Formation $( > 1 \ \mathrm { k m }$ thick) which consists mainly of dacitic and rhyolitic volcaniclastic rocks and minor amounts of mafic volcanic rocks, quartzites and quartz sericite schists. The Selkirk Formation also hosts the Phoenix, Selkirk and Tekwane meta-gabbronoritic intrusions and the Sikukwe meta-peridotite intrusion (Fig. 1b).

evidence.text: The relatively undeformed nature of the silicate host rocks to the ores can be explained by the fact that the silicate rocks are brittle...This can result in large blocks of undeformed gabbro and occasional narrow shear zones in the gabbro where the deformation has been absorbed.
evidence.paragraph_text: The metal distributions in the Tati ores can be understood by considering Laser-ICP data of magmatic sulfides from Phoenix (Van Geffen 2004). These data indicate that most of the Pd is hosted in pentlandite, whereas the bulk of the Os, Ir, Ru and Rh are hosted in pyrrhotite. Chalcopyrite has low concentrations of all PGE. Platinum is not accommodated by any of the base metal sulfides and may form PGM. As Pd is incompatible with regard to mss and iss (Barnes et al. 1997; Peregoedova 1998), the metal must have diffused from late crystallising magmatic sulfides into the pentlandite. As pentlandite exsolves from pyrrhotite at ca $6 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (Cabri 1992), the diffusion must have occurred below ca $6 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . At this stage, the various magmatic sulfide minerals were apparently still in physical contact. The formation of distinct Ni- and Pd-rich and Cu-rich massive sulfides must have occurred later, possibly in response to tectonism during granite intrusion. Magmatic sulfides behave in a variably ductile manner in a stress regime (Vokes 1969). Ductility decreases from chalcopyrite through pyrrhotite to pentlandite. As a result, tectonised ore bodies can have relatively chalcopyrite-, pyrrhotite- and pentlandite-rich portions (Barrett et al. 1977). The relatively undeformed nature of the silicate host rocks to the ores (Fig. 2a) can be explained by the fact that the silicate rocks are brittle, and hence, will behave more competently than the sulfides. This can result in large blocks of undeformed gabbro and occasional narrow shear zones in the gabbro where the deformation has been absorbed.

evidence.text: The estimated age of metamorphism is 600 - Ma
evidence.paragraph_text: # Genetic model and key criteria for exploration in the area In the Wadi Allaqi district, the volcano-sedimentary sequence and ophiolites (Fig. 2) were generally metamorphosed under greenschist facies conditions, but peak metamorphic conditions were considerably higher at depth up to $5 0 0 { - } 5 6 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and 5.5–6.3 kbar (Abd El-Naby and Frisch 2002). The peak metamorphism occurred coeval with the compressive deformation, manifested by regional folding and sinistral shearing (Zoheir and Klemm 2007). The estimated age of metamorphism is $6 0 0 { - } 5 8 5 \ \mathrm { M a }$ , comparable with granite magmatism in the area (Abd El-Naby and Frisch 2002). Hydrated and carbonated metabasic rocks at depth could have devolatilized when metamorphic conditions crossed the greenschist–amphibolite facies boundary. This process may have resulted in the liberation of low salinity aqueous-carbonic fluid with reduced sulfur and complexed Au (e.g., Phillips 1993; Phillips and Powell 2010). This auriferous fluid could have mixed with other fluid types, especially hot magmatic fluids at depth. The overlap of metamorphism with granite magmatism in the area may imply possible contributions from magmatic fluids. This may explain the S isotope composition of hydrothermal vein sulfides that is very dissimilar from pyrite in the host rocks. In the Um Garayat mine area, Au-bearing fluids likely migrated upward into the crust via D3 shear zones to a crustal level of about $6 { - } 8 \ \mathrm { k m }$ (Zoheir 2008b; Emam and Zoheir 2013). The geometry of the intersecting shear zones and lithological contacts allowed physical and geochemical preference for the gold-bearing fluids to deposit economic mineralization in some areas (e.g., Um Garayat, Marahiq, and Nile Valley Block).
evidence.document_title: Trace elements and isotope data of the Um Garayat gold deposit, Wadi Allaqi district, Egypt

evidence.paragraph_text: The Wadi Haimur–Abu Swayel area is characterized by the presence of three distinct rock units: ophiolitic rocks, gneisses and granitoids. The ophiolitic rocks (amphibolites, metagabbros, hornblendites and altered ultramafic rocks) have been studied previously by Abd El-Naby et al. (2000), who concluded that the amphibolites, metagabbros and hornblendites represent the metamorphic sole of the Wadi Haimur ophiolite belt. They also interpreted the overlying altered ultramafic rocks to represent overthrusted mantle peridotite. Thermobarometric results for the top part of the metamorphic sole indicate temperatures of $\approx 7 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and pressures between 5 and 8.5 Kbar, whereas the lower part suggest temperatures of $4 5 0 { \mathrm { - } } 6 2 0 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and pressures from 4.7 to 7.8 Kbar. The above authors concluded that the sole is the product of a dynamothermal metamorphism associated with the tectonic displacement of ultramafic rocks. Abd El-Naby et al. (2000) reported $\mathrm { S m / N d }$ isotopic ages from amphibolites and metagabbros in the Wadi Haimur area between 590 and $6 3 0 ~ \mathrm { M a }$ . They interpreted the age of $6 3 0 \ \mathrm { \ M a }$ as a lower age limit for ophiolite formation and an upper age limit for metamorphism, whereas the $5 9 0 \ \mathrm { \ m a }$ was interpreted as reflecting a cooling age. Trace element data from amphibolites of the Wadi Haimur–Abu Swayel area shows a transitional mid-ocean ridge basaltic to island arc tholeiitic affinity, which suggest a back-arc tectonic setting (Abd El-Naby and Frisch, 1999). The granitoid rocks of the mapped area are classified as tonalites and granites. The tonalites have compositions consistent with hydrous partial melting of a mafic source, suggesting subduction-related magmatism, whereas granites are correlated with collision-related magmatism (Abd El-Naby, 1998).

evidence.text: The upper age of metamorphism of the Wadi Haimur ophiolites is based on similar Sm/Nd ages
evidence.paragraph_text: Abd El-Naby et al. (2000) suggested that metamorphism occurred during terrane collision and ophiolite emplacement. Finger and Helmy (1998) suggested that the high-grade paragneisses in the Abu Swayel area were formed during collisional crustal thickening, when a Pan-African terrane assembly was attached to the eastern Sahara Craton at $\sim 6 5 0 -$ $6 0 0 \ \mathrm { M a }$ . Abd El-Naby and Frisch (2002) suggested that the protolithic sediments of the Wadi Haimur-Abu Swayel gneisses were evolved in a back-arc basin. Biotite in migmatized gneisses gave a K/Ar age of ${ \sim } 5 8 5 \ \mathrm { M a }$ , which is interpreted as the cooling age following peak metamorphic conditions (Abd El-Naby and Frisch 2002). The upper age of metamorphism of the Wadi Haimur ophiolites is based on similar $\mathrm { S m / N d }$ ages of clinopyroxene and hornblende (ca. $6 3 0 ~ \mathrm { M a }$ ), while a younger $\mathrm { S m / N d }$ age for a garnet-bearing rock (ca. $5 9 0 ~ \mathrm { M a } )$ ) is interpreted as the cooling age (Abd ElNaby et al. 2000).

evidence.text: the sedimentary protoliths of the Wadi Haimur–Abu Swayel gneisses evolved in a back-arc basin
evidence.paragraph_text: In Wadi Haimur, gneisses overlie the amphibolites and metagabbros (Fig. 3a). Whereas, in Wadi Um Ghalaga, gneisses are overlaid by altered ultramafic rocks. The contact between them is generally faulted. As concluded by Abd El-Naby and Frisch (1999), amphibolites and metagabbros of the Wadi Haimur–Abu Swayel area show a transitional mid-ocean ridge basaltic to island arc tholeiitic affinity, which suggests a back-arc tectonic setting. From field relations and geochemistry, it is concluded that the sedimentary protoliths of the Wadi Haimur–Abu Swayel gneisses evolved in a back-arc basin.

evidence.text: These rocks cover an area of 470 km^2
evidence.paragraph_text: Gneisses represent the dominant rock type in the mapped area and are particularly well developed in its northern part (Fig. 2). These rocks cover an area of $\approx 4 7 0 ~ \mathrm { k m } ^ { 2 }$ and form high relief. They are intruded by granitoid rocks in the northern and southern part of the study area (Fig. 3b). The gneisses are generally fine- to mediumgrained and display dark to light grey colors. Lineation and foliation, common tectonic features in these rocks, are defined by the parallel alignment of biotite, hornblende and plagioclase aggregates. The gneisses are locally mylonitized in Wadi Haimur (Fig. 2). The Wadi Haimur–Abu Swayel gneisses are subdivided into biotite-gneiss, hornblende–biotite gneiss and garnet–biotite gneiss. The biotite-gneiss composed mainly of quartz $( 3 0 - 4 0 \ \mathrm { \ v o l . \% } )$ , plagioclase $( 1 6 - 2 8 \% )$ , biotite $( 2 6 - 3 0 \% )$ , hornblende $( 1 - 8 \% )$ , alkali – feldspar $( 1 - 3 \% )$ and muscovite $( 2 \mathrm { - } 4 \% )$ . The hornblende – biotite gneiss consists essentially of quartz $( 3 1 - 4 6 \% )$ , plagioclase $( 2 6 - 3 1 \% )$ , hornblende $( 1 2 - 2 0 \% )$ and biotite $( 5 - 1 5 \% )$ . The mylonitic garnet–biotite gneiss composed mainly of quartz $( 5 0 \% )$ , biotite $( 2 8 \% )$ , garnet $( 6 \% )$ , plagioclase $( 3 \% )$ and alkali-feldspar $( 3 \% )$ . Garnet appears as subhedral porphyroblasts $( 0 . 5 ~ \mathrm { c m }$ in diameter) intergrown with biotite, plagioclase and quartz (Fig. 3c). It encloses disoriented inclusions from quartz and biotite. Most of the K-feldspar in all varieties is altered to kaolinite, so that only a few crystals of microcline are preserved. Chlorite and epidote, which replace biotite and plagioclase, respectively, are secondary minerals. Zircon, apatite and ilmenite are present as accessories. * Total Fe as FeO. * * Alm, almandine; Pyp, pyrope; Grs, grossular; Sps, spessartine.

evidence.text: the gabbro and diorite ages (729 +/- 17 and 736 +/- Ma, respectively) in the Abu Swayel area
evidence.paragraph_text: Hashad et al. (1999) reported $\operatorname { R b } / \operatorname { S r }$ whole rock age of $6 7 2 \pm 1 5$ Ma from tonalite and quartz diorite of Gabal Murra (Fig. 1a). This age is younger than the gabbro and diorite ages $( 7 2 9 \pm$ 17 and $7 3 6 \pm 1 1$ Ma, respectively) in the Abu Swayel area, as dated by Kro¨ ner et al. (1992). Abd El Wahed (1998) reported $\operatorname { R b } / \operatorname { S r }$ whole rock age of $5 7 1 \pm 7$ Ma for the late collision granite in the Gabal Murra area.

evidence.text: a gabbro at Phoenix Mine was dated at 2,703 +/- Ma (electron microprobe dating of monazite grains)
evidence.paragraph_text: Tati greenstone belt, together with the adjacent Vumba greenstone belt, forms part of the Francistown Arc Complex (Carney et al. 1994; McCourt et al. 2004) located along the southwestern margin of the Zimbabwe craton (Fig. 1a). Based on lithostratigraphic similarities, Carney et al. (1994) correlated the volcano-sedimentary rocks of the Francistown Arc Complex with the ca 2.7 Ga Upper Bulawayan greenstones in Zimbabwe (Fig. 1a). Bagai et al. (2002) dated tonalite–trondhjemite gneisses in the Vumba greenstone belt at ${ 2 , 6 4 7 \pm 4 }$ to $2 { , } 6 9 6 { \pm } 4 \ \mathrm { M a }$ (U-Pb SHRIMP age on zircon), and Van Geffen (2004) dated a gabbro at Phoenix Mine at $2 , 7 0 3 { \pm } 3 0$ Ma (electron microprobe dating of monazite grains), consistent with this model.

evidence.text: The Wadi Haimur–Abu Swayel gneisses are subdivided into biotite-gneiss, hornblende–biotite gneiss and garnet–biotite gneiss.
evidence.paragraph_text: Gneisses represent the dominant rock type in the mapped area and are particularly well developed in its northern part (Fig. 2). These rocks cover an area of $\approx 4 7 0 ~ \mathrm { k m } ^ { 2 }$ and form high relief. They are intruded by granitoid rocks in the northern and southern part of the study area (Fig. 3b). The gneisses are generally fine- to mediumgrained and display dark to light grey colors. Lineation and foliation, common tectonic features in these rocks, are defined by the parallel alignment of biotite, hornblende and plagioclase aggregates. The gneisses are locally mylonitized in Wadi Haimur (Fig. 2). The Wadi Haimur–Abu Swayel gneisses are subdivided into biotite-gneiss, hornblende–biotite gneiss and garnet–biotite gneiss. The biotite-gneiss composed mainly of quartz $( 3 0 - 4 0 \ \mathrm { \ v o l . \% } )$ , plagioclase $( 1 6 - 2 8 \% )$ , biotite $( 2 6 - 3 0 \% )$ , hornblende $( 1 - 8 \% )$ , alkali – feldspar $( 1 - 3 \% )$ and muscovite $( 2 \mathrm { - } 4 \% )$ . The hornblende – biotite gneiss consists essentially of quartz $( 3 1 - 4 6 \% )$ , plagioclase $( 2 6 - 3 1 \% )$ , hornblende $( 1 2 - 2 0 \% )$ and biotite $( 5 - 1 5 \% )$ . The mylonitic garnet–biotite gneiss composed mainly of quartz $( 5 0 \% )$ , biotite $( 2 8 \% )$ , garnet $( 6 \% )$ , plagioclase $( 3 \% )$ and alkali-feldspar $( 3 \% )$ . Garnet appears as subhedral porphyroblasts $( 0 . 5 ~ \mathrm { c m }$ in diameter) intergrown with biotite, plagioclase and quartz (Fig. 3c). It encloses disoriented inclusions from quartz and biotite. Most of the K-feldspar in all varieties is altered to kaolinite, so that only a few crystals of microcline are preserved. Chlorite and epidote, which replace biotite and plagioclase, respectively, are secondary minerals. Zircon, apatite and ilmenite are present as accessories. * Total Fe as FeO. * * Alm, almandine; Pyp, pyrope; Grs, grossular; Sps, spessartine.

evidence.text: The prospects are hosted in late Precambrian mafic–ultramafic rocks
evidence.paragraph_text: Melonite group minerals and other tellurides are described from three Cu–Ni–PGE prospects in the Eastern Desert of Egypt: Gabbro Akarem, Genina Gharbia and Abu Swayel. The prospects are hosted in late Precambrian mafic–ultramafic rocks and have different geologic histories. The Gabbro Akarem prospect is hosted in dunite pipes where net-textured and massive sulfides are associated with spinel and $\mathrm { C r }$ -magnetite. Michenerite, merenskyite, Pd–Bi melonite and hessite occur mainly as inclusions in sulfides. Typical magmatic textures indicate a limited role of late- and post-magmatic hydrothermal processes. At Genina Gharbia, ore forms either disseminations in peridotite or massive patches in hornblende-gabbro in the vicinity of metasedimentary rocks. Actinolitic hornblende, epidote, chlorite and quartz are common secondary silicates.
evidence.document_title: Melonite group minerals and other tellurides from three Cu–Ni–PGE prospects, Eastern Desert, Egypt

evidence.text: Net-textured and massive sulfides in dunite pipes comprise pyrrhotite, pentlandite, chalcopyrite, cubanite, and pyrite
evidence.paragraph_text: Net-textured and massive sulfides (Fig. 6A) in dunite pipes comprise pyrrhotite, pentlandite, chalcopyrite, cubanite, and pyrite in decreasing order of abundance (see also Sharara et al. 1999). Pyrrhotite forms large $( 0 . 5 { - } 2 ~ \mathrm { m m } )$ anhedral crystals interstitial to olivine and orthopyroxene; in places, it may contain euhedral inclusions of $\mathrm { C r }$ -magnetite (Fig. 6B). Pentlandite forms either rounded crystals included in pyrrhotite or veinlets along cracks in pyrrhotite. Exsolution flames (Fig. 6C) of pentlandite occur at grain boundaries of pyrrhotite and along microfractures in pyrrhotite. In places, pentlandite may be replaced by violarite (Fig. 6D) and mackinawite. Chalcopyrite occurs as anhedral grains at the outer margins of pyrrhotite and as veinlets crosscutting massive sulfides and in serpentinized olivine. Pyrite is found in micro shear zones and in secondary serpentine-magnesite veinlets crosscutting massive sulfides.
evidence.document_title: Gabbro Akarem, Eastern Desert, Egypt: Cu-Ni-PGE mineralization in a concentrically zoned mafic-ultramafic complex

evidence.text: Sm/Nd dating of whole rock-metamorphic mineral pairs from ophiolitic amphibolite and metagabbro
evidence.paragraph_text: The ophiolitic, gneissic and granitic rocks of the region lack definitive age determinations. Abd El-Naby et al. (2000) reported $\mathrm { S m / N d }$ dating of whole rock-metamorphic mineral pairs from ophiolitic amphibolite and metagabbro in Wadi Haimur–Abu Swayel area. The hornblende–, clinopyroxene–whole rock pairs yield an age of $6 3 3 \pm 4 2 \mathrm { \ M a }$ , which is older than that of garnet– whole rock pair $( 5 9 2 \pm 3 \mathrm { \ M a } )$ . They interpreted the $6 3 3 \pm 4 2$ Ma age as the upper age limit of the metamorphism, whereas the younger $5 9 2 \pm 3$ Ma age for the garnet-bearing rock as reflecting a cooling age. The $6 3 3 \pm 4 2$ Ma is in agreement with the metamorphic age ( $\approx 6 2 0 { - } 6 5 0 \ \mathrm { \ M a } )$ ) of monazites from the metapelitic paragneisses around Abu Swayel mine, determined by means of chemical $\mathrm { T h ( U ) { - } P b }$ dating with electron microprobe (Finger and Helmy, 1998). These ages are significantly lower than the age of ophiolite formation in the Abu Swayel area ( $\approx 7 3 0 \ \mathrm { M a }$ ), as reported by Kro¨ ner et al. (1992).

evidence.text: This phase of metamorphism is reflected by thermobarometric studies of the sole rocks
evidence.paragraph_text: 2. Island arc collision/accretion phase: it led to imbricate thrusting of the overriding oceanic plate represented by altered ultramafic rocks over back-arc oceanic crust represented by the units of the metamorphic sole (Fig. 7b) and an early peak of medium grade metamorphism at $\approx 6 3 0$ Ma. This phase of metamorphism is reflected by thermobarometric studies of the sole rocks (Abd El-Naby et al., 2000) and metamorphic age of monazites ( $\approx 6 2 0 – 6 5 0$ Ma) from the metapelitic paragneisses around Abu Swayel mine (Finger and Helmy, 1998). Crustal thickening, as a result of collision, was accompanied by granitoid intrusions and a late phase of low grade metamorphism around $6 0 0 \ \mathrm { M a }$ ago, as reflected by the $\mathrm { K } / \mathrm { A r }$ ages of the studied gneisses (Fig. 7c). This phase was followed by denudation faulting, extension and uplifting.

evidence.text: the mylonitic–garnet gneiss (sample 5A) displays a distinctly higher FeO content (30 - 34 %)
evidence.paragraph_text: The plagioclase is generally oligoclase and in sample 141, andesine is present. A distinct compositional difference occurs between biotite from non-mylonitic gneisses and the mylonitic–garnet gneiss. The biotites of the non-mylonitic gneisses are $\mathrm { M g \mathrm { - } }$ and $\mathrm { F e } ^ { 2 + }$ -biotites, while the mylonitic – garnet gneiss (sample 5A) displays a distinctly higher FeO content $( 3 0 - 3 4 \% )$ and lies close to the field of siderophyllite and lepidomelane. Biotite contains an average of $2 . 7 4 \ \mathrm { w t . \% }$ $\mathrm { T i O } _ { 2 }$ . The garnets from mylonitic gneiss sample are typically almandine-rich. A garnet zoning profile (Fig. 5) shows compositional zoning with the core enriched in the spessartine component relative to the rims, while the almandine component increases from core to rim. This type of zoning is characteristic of garnets growing during prograde metamorphism. The grossular and pyrope contents, as well as $\mathrm { F e } / ( \mathrm { F e } + \mathrm { M g } )$ ratio, show no systematic change across the crystal. Based on the classification of Hey (1954), the chlorite has the composition of ripidolite and pycnochlorite. The white mica from gneiss samples 156, 78 and 145 is characterized by a low paragonite component and moderate phengite component.

evidence.text: in the carbonatized metasiltstone with carbonaceous laminae (sample G7)
evidence.paragraph_text: Recrystallized pyrite in the carbonatized metasiltstone with carbonaceous laminae (sample G7) has noticeable contents of Cu (up to 2881 ppm), Ni (up to $1 2 1 2 \ \mathrm { p p m }$ ), Co (up to $9 9 7 \ \mathrm { p p m } ,$ ), Pb (up to $8 2 9 ~ \mathrm { p p m }$ ), As (up to $3 0 8 ~ \mathrm { p p m }$ ), Se (up to $1 8 1 \ \mathrm { p p m }$ ), Zn (up to $4 9 \mathrm { p p m }$ ), Te (up to $3 7 \mathrm { p p m }$ ), Bi (up to $3 1 \mathrm { p p m } )$ , and $\mathrm { A g }$ (up to $2 3 \mathrm { p p m }$ ). Gold contents in this pyrite variety are less than 1 ppm or below the detection limit. The single analysis in which the Au content measures $0 . 9 8 \mathrm { p p m }$ i s enriched in most trace elements (Co, Ni, As, Se, Ag, Te, and Bi) compared to the analyses in which Au is absent or very low. In sample 22b, the weakly recrystallized pyrite shows high concentrations of As (up to $2 6 1 4 ~ \mathrm { p p m }$ ), Pb (max. $1 1 2 5 \ \mathrm { p p m } )$ ), Co (max. $4 6 4 ~ \mathrm { p p m }$ ), Sb (max. $3 0 3 \ \mathrm { p p m }$ ), Se (max. $1 5 4 ~ \mathrm { p p m }$ ), Ni (max. $1 3 8 ~ \mathrm { p p m }$ ), Cu (max. 77 ppm), Zn and $\mathrm { H g }$ (max. $2 3 \ \mathrm { p p m }$ . Molybdenum, Cr, and V concentrations are still measurable (up to $2 0 \mathrm { p p m }$ ), while gold content is very low $( \leq 0 . 2 \ \mathrm { p p m } )$ .

evidence.text: Pyrite disseminated in carbonatized wallrock (sample G7)
evidence.paragraph_text: # ${ \delta } ^ { 3 4 } 5$ values of sulfides The sulfur isotope data show two distinct groups. In samples G4 and G5, $\delta ^ { 3 4 } \mathrm { S }$ values in pyrite, pyrrhotite, and chalcopyrite are comparable, $0 . 7 { - } 3 . 7 \text{‰}$ (ESM Table 6). In sample G7, $\dot { 8 } ^ { 3 4 } \mathrm { S }$ values in pyrite range from $- 9 . 3$ to $- 1 0 . 6 \text{‰}$ . Disseminated large euhedral crystals of pyrite give $\delta ^ { 3 4 } \mathrm { S }$ values of $2 . 1 - 3 . 7 \text{‰}$ . In some individual crystals, the $\delta ^ { 3 4 } \mathrm { S }$ values of rims (3.3 and $3 . 7 \text{‰}$ are consistently higher than those of the cores (2.1 and $2 . 3 \text{‰}$ ). There is no systematic variation in the $\delta ^ { 3 4 } \mathrm { S }$ value between pyrite and pyrrhotite, though microscopic investigation showed replacement of pyrite-arsenopyrite by pyrrhotite and chalcopyrite. The $\delta ^ { 3 4 } \mathrm { S }$ values of isolated crystals of pyrrhotite in two samples are nearly identical $( 2 . 0 - 2 . 6 \% o )$ . The $\delta ^ { 3 4 } \mathrm { S }$ values of pyrrhotite overgrowths on pyrite and arsenopyrite in samples G4 and G5 are variable $( 0 . 7 - 2 . 9 \text{‰}$ . Only one chalcopyrite grain is analyzed for its S isotope composition, which is clearly comparable to the $\delta ^ { 3 4 } \mathrm { S }$ value of the adjacent pyrite $( 2 . 4 \text{‰}$ . Pyrite disseminated in carbonatized wallrock (sample G7) shows no distinct isotopic variations. The euhedral recrystallized cores were too small for ablation.

evidence.paragraph_text: The intrusion hosting the Phikwe deposit forms part of the Selebi-Phikwe belt of intrusions that also contain the Selebi, Selebi North, Dikoloti, Lentswe and Phokoje deposits (Fig. 1e–g). In all these deposits, the sulfide ores are mainly associated with boudinaged lenses and layers of fine- to medium-grained amphibolite interlayered with various types of gneisses (Gordon 1973; Wakefield 1976; Key 1976; Gallon 1986; Brown 1988). The ore-bearing intrusions are generally relatively thin (e.g. on average 11 m in the Phikwe area; Lear 1979), but this may largely be the result of intense folding and shearing. The amphibolites consist mainly of hornblende, feldspar, gedrite and mica. Minor metamorphic orthopyroxene and olivine also occur. Based on whole rock compositional data and CIPW norms of a large number of samples, Brown (1988) estimated that the parental magmas to the intrusions were tholeiitic basalts (with ca $8 \ \mathrm { \ w t { \% } \ M g O } ,$ that crystallised variable proportions of olivine $\left( \mathrm { F o } _ { 8 5 } \right)$ , pyroxene $\left( \mathrm { E n } _ { 8 5 } \right)$ and plagioclase $\left( \mathsf { A n } _ { 8 3 } \right)$ .
evidence.document_title: The composition of magmatic Ni–Cu–(PGE) sulfide deposits in the Tati and Selebi-Phikwe belts of eastern Botswana

evidence.paragraph_text: The Wadi Haimur–Abu Swayel area is characterized by the presence of three distinct rock units: ophiolitic rocks, gneisses and granitoids. The ophiolitic rocks (amphibolites, metagabbros, hornblendites and altered ultramafic rocks) have been studied previously by Abd El-Naby et al. (2000), who concluded that the amphibolites, metagabbros and hornblendites represent the metamorphic sole of the Wadi Haimur ophiolite belt. They also interpreted the overlying altered ultramafic rocks to represent overthrusted mantle peridotite. Thermobarometric results for the top part of the metamorphic sole indicate temperatures of $\approx 7 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and pressures between 5 and 8.5 Kbar, whereas the lower part suggest temperatures of $4 5 0 { \mathrm { - } } 6 2 0 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and pressures from 4.7 to 7.8 Kbar. The above authors concluded that the sole is the product of a dynamothermal metamorphism associated with the tectonic displacement of ultramafic rocks. Abd El-Naby et al. (2000) reported $\mathrm { S m / N d }$ isotopic ages from amphibolites and metagabbros in the Wadi Haimur area between 590 and $6 3 0 ~ \mathrm { M a }$ . They interpreted the age of $6 3 0 \ \mathrm { \ M a }$ as a lower age limit for ophiolite formation and an upper age limit for metamorphism, whereas the $5 9 0 \ \mathrm { \ m a }$ was interpreted as reflecting a cooling age. Trace element data from amphibolites of the Wadi Haimur–Abu Swayel area shows a transitional mid-ocean ridge basaltic to island arc tholeiitic affinity, which suggest a back-arc tectonic setting (Abd El-Naby and Frisch, 1999). The granitoid rocks of the mapped area are classified as tonalites and granites. The tonalites have compositions consistent with hydrous partial melting of a mafic source, suggesting subduction-related magmatism, whereas granites are correlated with collision-related magmatism (Abd El-Naby, 1998).
evidence.document_title: Origin of the Wadi Haimur–Abu Swayel gneiss belt, south Eastern Desert, Egypt: petrological and geochronological constraints

edges.source: zone of massive sulfides and sulfide stringers

evidence.paragraph_text: The Abu Swayel area is covered by metasedimentary rocks, metamorphosed mafic–ultramafic rocks and non-metamorphosed granites. These sulfide-bearing mafic–ultramafic rocks are metamorphosed to amphibolite facies conditions ( $6 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and 4 to 5 kbar; Helmy, 1996). In the Abu Swayel mine, the mineralized mafic–ultramafic rock is a lenticular sheet-like body, $5 0 0 ~ \mathrm { ~ m ~ }$ long and ca. $5 0 \textrm { m }$ wide, which strikes NW–SE and dips $6 5 ^ { \circ }$ to $7 5 ^ { \circ }$ to the NE, following the regional structures of the enclosing biotite schist. The mineralized mafic–ultramafic body comprises two types of amphibole rocks; coarsegrained in the hanging wall and medium-grained in the footwall (Fig. 6). The contact between the two subunits is controlled by a syn-metamorphic shear zone dipping $6 5 ^ { \circ }$ to $7 0 ^ { \circ }$ to the NE. Sulfides were redistributed during amphibolite facies metamorphism and syn-metamorphic shearing and a zone of massive sulfides and sulfide stringers enriched in Cu formed along the shear plane (Helmy, 1996). Feasibility studies of the sulfide ore comprise a vertical shaft to a depth of $6 9 \mathrm { m }$ , a horizontal adit $2 2 ~ \mathrm { m }$ long at a depth of $2 0 ~ \mathrm { m }$ and 11 drill cores. Samples for this study were collected from dump and the adit intersecting the sulfide ore.
evidence.document_title: Melonite group minerals and other tellurides from three Cu–Ni–PGE prospects, Eastern Desert, Egypt
